author: @diabsiniman
text: suggestion: use some more of that shelving material to make and "A" frame that the back wheel slots into, that will stop a lot of your sliding around problems. very nice video.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks for the tip! Im actually working on something similar for the next video. :)

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: LOL, Robert, I thought your channel got hacked. I was like who is this? lol then I remembered him being on here before. He must be your younger self.

author: @RussellDuffer
text: You might put some braces from the frame up to your bike's rear axle, to both keep the bike balanced and to keep the frame from sliding under the wheels.

author: @nickhadziannis8451
text:
Luke old bean🖖🏿..... long time you have not been seen...🖖

I'm looking forward to seeing what you purpose to make(hopefully ur not building an exercise machine).

Observations; looks(sounds like) like alot of resistance from the rollers...

Mabey you could try(cos I think I know  where ur going with this, but would be pleased if I was wrong and u show me somthing new)   mounting a triangle frame on each side for the wheel  axle' or to brace the frame of the bike on.... so when your bum/body weight is on the bike it squashes the tires into the rollers 50% of the way...   right now almost all the bike weight is bieng expressed through to two touch points on the rolers.  Obviously this would also increase your stability.

If your wheel bobbed/ hovered on the rollers   , then less tork would be expressed as vibrations through the roller axis and through your printer bearings.  

This would allow you to achieve higher speeds with less effort expended on your part to overcome the friction feedback in the form of vibrations.... from the rollers taking the weight whilst also siphoning off the kinetic energy you apply through the peddles....

I hope your.gona make some kind of generator.... and get Fred Dibna to help you outfit it with some coils and magnets.....

I eagerly anticipate the next post.... chop chop...
Signed: vestri auditorium

author: @jorgemartin7697
text:
And if you connect it to a generator to charge some batteries?
I think the rollers are too wide I don't think that's right and the front wheel should be locked and attached to the rear platform, to stabilize the bike,

author: @Graham_Wideman
text: I'll float a theory that the print-roller designers did not select the axle or bearings for a hundred of more kg of load, and rotation speeds that fast. So I'm expecting this setup not to last very long.  But nothing lost trying it out!

author: @bohabdestructo7489
text: Treadmill rollers are also a good option, especially if you want to make electricity with it. The front roller has a pulley that is attached with a belt to a motor that can be used to make electricity.

author: @michaeltucker8645
text: Find you an old automobile jack stand pull the chuck out of it and cut the top so your frame sits in  it and be alot easier to balance and free . Put you a bit of the area rug none slip material under the roller  it wont move at all then . Great vid

replies.author: @michaeltucker8645
replies.text: I'd just make a stand for the bike a reuse a treadmill  belt drive since I have a few available. Could cut the drive belt down so it's not taking up alot if room

replies.author: @michaeltucker8645
replies.text: How a stand holding the bike still on top of a conveyer belt from an old treadmill. I didn't say lock the wheels in place

replies.author: @michaeltucker8645
replies.text: Think of 2 arms on a working treadmill that can lock onto a bike. Then just rid the treadmill of its motors and electronics. It's a very stable way to pedal in place. You could even put a smaller motor on the the conveyor belt drive and generate power
